6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few years ago, recommending a System Design course was easy. If someone asked me where to start, I’d almost always point them toward Educative’s Grokking the System Design Interview. It was one of the first resources that took what felt like an intimidating, open-ended interview topic and turned it into a structured learning path.
Fast forward to 2026, and the landscape looks completely different. There are dozens of competing courses, YouTube channels, AI tutors, newsletters, books, and interview platforms. Every new course claims to be more modern, more comprehensive, or better aligned with today’s interviews.
That naturally raises an important question: Is the original Grokking the System Design Interview still worth buying, or has it been overtaken by newer alternatives?
After spending weeks comparing today’s most popular System Design resources and revisiting the original course, I think the answer is still yes, but with a few important caveats.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What the Original Grokking Course Actually Is
One thing I noticed while reading reviews online is that many people talk about the course without clearly explaining what it tries to accomplish.
This isn’t a distributed systems textbook, nor is it a complete software architecture curriculum. It’s an interview preparation course designed to teach engineers how to approach open-ended System Design problems. Rather than assuming you already know how to structure an interview, it gradually introduces the building blocks of distributed systems before combining them into larger design exercises. That structured approach is one of the reasons the course became so influential and helped popularize the “Grokking” style of interview preparation.
If you’re expecting an exhaustive reference covering every architecture pattern in existence, you’ll probably be disappointed. If you’re looking for a structured roadmap into System Design interviews, the course is much closer to its intended purpose.
What I Looked For During My Review
I wasn’t interested in comparing marketing pages or counting the number of lessons. Instead, I evaluated the course using the same questions I ask whenever I mentor engineers preparing for interviews.
Can someone finish this course and confidently approach a System Design interview?
Does it teach architectural thinking instead of memorization?
Will the material still be useful after the interview?
Those questions matter far more than whether the course contains twenty or fifty hours of content.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What the Course Does Really Well
The biggest strength of the original Grokking course is its structure.
Many System Design resources immediately jump into designing YouTube, Instagram, Uber, or Twitter. Those examples are interesting, but they often present finished architectures without spending enough time explaining how those decisions were made.
Educative takes a different approach.
Instead of starting with complete systems, it begins by teaching the individual building blocks that appear repeatedly in distributed systems. Topics like caching, load balancing, partitioning, replication, consistency, and databases are introduced first before gradually appearing inside larger case studies. That progression makes it much easier to understand why architectural decisions are made instead of simply copying reference diagrams.
In my experience, that’s exactly what interviewers want to see. They care much more about your reasoning process than whether your final diagram looks identical to one you’ve memorized.
It Teaches a Framework, Not Just Solutions
One thing I appreciated after revisiting the course is that it focuses on developing a repeatable way of thinking.
Every interview starts differently.

• Sometimes you’ll be asked to design a messaging platform.

• Sometimes it’s a recommendation system.

• Sometimes it’s an API or a URL shortener.
The technologies change, but the interview process rarely does.
You still need to clarify requirements, estimate scale, identify bottlenecks, discuss trade-offs, and evolve the design step by step.
The course reinforces that framework throughout the curriculum, and I think that’s the biggest reason it has remained relevant for so many years. Even though distributed systems continue evolving, the underlying engineering thought process hasn’t changed nearly as much.
Where It Shows Its Age
No course remains perfect forever, and the original Grokking course isn’t an exception.
Compared with some newer platforms, the presentation feels less polished in places. Several competitors now include more animations, interactive whiteboards, or highly produced videos. If you’re someone who prefers learning almost entirely through video, you may find Educative’s text-first style takes a little getting used to. The course has expanded its video content over time, but its interactive reading format is still a defining characteristic.
The course also isn’t intended to be the final word on System Design. If you’re interviewing for senior staff or principal engineering roles, you’ll almost certainly need additional resources covering advanced distributed systems, production architecture, and deeper engineering trade-offs.
Those are simply a reminder that the course has a clearly defined scope.
How It Compares to Today’s Alternatives
One thing that surprised me while comparing courses is that most resources aren’t really trying to replace each other anymore. Instead, they solve different parts of the learning process.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Educative gave me the roadmap.
Fenzo.ai became the place where I explored difficult topics more deeply by asking additional questions.
Books helped me build stronger engineering intuition.
One Example That Stood Out
Caching ended up being one of the best examples of why I still recommend the course.
Most resources explain where Redis fits into a System Design diagram.
That’s useful.
But it doesn’t tell you why caching becomes necessary, what consistency problems it introduces, or when you shouldn’t use it.
Educative spends much more time discussing those trade-offs, which is exactly where real System Design interviews tend to spend their time.
After finishing the caching module, I opened Fenzo.ai and started asking additional questions about cache invalidation, eviction strategies, and consistency models. That combination of structured learning followed by interactive exploration helped me understand the topic far better than simply watching another architecture walkthrough.
Who Should Buy This Course?
I think the original Grokking course is an excellent investment if you’re preparing for software engineering interviews where System Design is part of the hiring process. It’s also a great choice if you’ve spent a few years building backend systems but want a more structured understanding of distributed architecture.
On the other hand, if you’re already designing large-scale production systems every day or interviewing for principal-level engineering roles, this probably shouldn’t be your only resource. At that point, you’ll benefit much more from advanced books, architecture case studies, and real production experience alongside interview preparation. That matches the course’s intended scope rather than being a limitation.
My Biggest Takeaway
After comparing so many System Design resources, one realization kept coming back to me.
The newest course isn’t automatically the best course.
Good engineering education tends to age much more slowly than technology itself.
Distributed systems evolve, cloud providers launch new services, and AI changes how we learn, but the core engineering trade-offs remain remarkably consistent. That’s why the original Grokking course continues to hold up. It teaches you how to think through architectural decisions rather than simply introducing another collection of technologies.
That mindset is valuable whether you’re preparing for interviews or designing real software.
Final Verdict
So, is the original Grokking the System Design Interview on Educative worth it in 2026?
For most software engineers preparing for System Design interviews, I’d say yes.
Not because it’s the newest course.
Not because it has the flashiest interface.
But because it still provides one of the clearest, most structured frameworks for learning how to approach System Design problems.
If someone asked me for one structured course to start with, the original Grokking course would still be the recommendation I’d make.
